candidates.direction_name: Reopen The PTX One-K 128x128 Hot-Band Control Branch
ranking_notes: The immediately preceding copy-cadence retune already regressed, so another in-family retune should not stay ranked first.
This branch is orthogonal while still preserving the current 128x128 geometry and split.
The prior PTX measurement was already near the current control run, which makes a quick A/B round plausible.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 1/20 already spent the first budget unit on a bounded retune of the active one-K 128x128 copy cadence, and node_a measured that branch slightly slower at 25.381776 ms versus the 25.325055 ms accepted base. The dominant hot-band kernel still owns about 32.918752 ms of device time with 196 registers per thread, only 16.65% active warps, 47.93% tensor-pipe activity, 7.60% long-scoreboard stall, and 5.75% barrier stall. The PTX one-K control branch keeps the same grouped-row 128x128 grid and hot-band split, but swaps the inner accumulate/export control path. That makes it the cleanest orthogonal next test because a prior PTX measurement already landed at 25.379312 ms, close enough that a small control-path win could be real on this surface.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band inner-loop control and export overhead in the standard one-K 128x128 path, rather than the peeled 64x384 residual rows or the 64x96 tail.
candidates.risk: Moderate to high. The PTX WMMA path is correctness-sensitive, but it is already present in-tree and the implementation surface should stay small because node_c only needs to route the default hot-band launch onto the existing branch.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the current 25.381776 ms run and the prior PTX 25.379312 ms run
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the PTX one-K branch does not beat the current 25.381776 ms run cleanly or if it regresses correctness/build despite staying on the same hot-band geometry.

candidates.direction_name: Promote The Existing 256x128 Pivot Hot-Band Kernel
ranking_notes: This is the clearest geometry pivot still sitting in-tree and ready to route.
It is ranked below the PTX branch because the implementation surface and behavior change are both larger.
If the PTX path stays flat, this becomes the next serious family to try.
candidates.hypothesis: If the current 4-warp 128x128 branch has saturated its control path, the next structurally different branch should test geometry, not another wait-order micro-tweak. The dominant kernel is still register-limited to two resident CTAs with only 16.65% active warps, and it launches a 60x50 grid over the first 6400 x 7680 hot-band rows. The in-tree 256x128 pivot family restores an 8-warp CTA while halving the pivot-band CTA count, so it is the cleanest way to test whether CTA geometry and block-count overhead are now the limiting factor on the hot band.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: CTA geometry and block-count overhead on the hot-band region, rather than a pure one-K copy-cadence problem.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The kernel already exists, but routing the default path onto it changes the hot-band family more materially than the PTX branch and could trade lower CTA count for a worse live set.
metrics_to_recheck: hot-band kernel name and grid size in ncu_details.csv
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 256x128 pivot branch cannot beat the current one-K 128x128 control run or if its larger CTA shape lowers active warps without improving hot-band time.

candidates.direction_name: Retune Hot-Band CTA Traversal On The 128x128 Grid
ranking_notes: This branch is intentionally cheap and reversible.
It stays below the PTX and 256x128 candidates because prior CTA-order ideas have had mixed evidence.
Keep it alive as the small-surface fallback if both larger hot-band branches stall.
candidates.hypothesis: The current default hot-band branch already remaps physical CTAs into grouped logical tiles, but it still launches a large 60x50 grid while only two 4-warp CTAs can reside at once. In that low-occupancy regime, a lighter grouped-row or serpentine traversal on the 128x128 grid may recover a small amount of inter-CTA B-tile locality without changing the math kernel, registers, or shared-memory footprint. This is a cheap locality probe that stays orthogonal to both the PTX control branch and the 256x128 geometry branch.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA locality and block traversal efficiency on the hot-band grid under register-limited low occupancy.
candidates.risk: Low to moderate. The change surface is small, but previous heavier CTA-order experiments on other hot-band families were not strong wins, so this stays as a lower-priority branch.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if CTA traversal changes leave hot-band time and L2-facing counters flat after one round; do not spend repeated rounds on launch-order tuning.

The existing 256x128 pivot family halves the pivot-band CTA count and restores an 8-warp CTA, so it is the cleanest next test of whether hot-band geometry and block-count overhead are now the limiting factor.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band CTA geometry and block-count overhead on the current 128x128 PTX base, rather than a remaining local control-path bubble inside the winning branch.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The kernel already exists in-tree, but routing the default path onto it changes the hot-band family more materially than another PTX micro-retune and could lose if the larger CTA worsens the live set.

candidates.direction_name: Trim The Grouped-Row 128x128 Sibling Export Scratch To The PTX-Style Single Stage
ranking_notes: Historical run 20260420_185423_bf16_gemm_v1_1181247 measured 24.422464 ms, 0.244801 ms off the current accepted base 24.177664 ms.
Rehydrated from round_history because this family is absent from the current live frontier after the policy update.
Original diagnosis diagnosis_20260420_184841 ranked this direction at #1.
candidates.hypothesis: The latest measured run 20260420_184822_bf16_gemm_v1_2ab9365 is effectively at parity with the accepted 2e4dd24 best: 24.44902420 ms versus 24.44441605 ms, with the hot-band kernel also nearly identical at 32.831584 ms versus 32.801760 ms. The new sibling path is already correct and preserves grouped-row traversal, but it still carries the generic two-stage `c_shared` export path: 26,112 B shared per block versus 22,016 B on the accepted PTX microkernel. The cleanest next move is to keep this sibling base and port the PTX-style single-stage per-warp export scratch lifetime into its store path, so the remaining gap can be chased through a bounded shared/export trim instead of another family pivot.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: The likely residual bottleneck is shared export/writeback overhead rather than math scheduling. If the single-stage export trim works, it should lower the sibling's shared footprint and reduce the tiny hot-band gap without harming the already-matched barrier and long-scoreboard profile.
candidates.risk: Low-medium. This is a narrow refinement on a correct near-best base, but it touches the export path, so the main risk is reintroducing subtle writeback or lifetime bugs while chasing a single-digit-microsecond gain.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the export-scratch trim causes any correctness regression or if it cannot beat the current 24.44902420 ms sibling result while keeping the hot-band kernel within the current near-parity range.

The dominant hot-band kernel itself got markedly worse versus the restored accepted PTX branch: kernel time rose from 32.97 ms to 39.57 ms, registers climbed from 200 to 212, shared memory rose from 22.016 KiB to 43.008 KiB, tensor activity fell from 47.72% to 40.05%, and barrier stall jumped from 5.52% to 10.04%. Because the implementation surface has already been restored to the accepted `da1a5bb` PTX microkernel, the best next move is not another structural hot-band rewrite but the strongest already-measured PTX-adjacent fallback on that surface: restore the 4-row grouped traversal window, which earlier beat the current restored base at 25.40644836 ms while staying inside the same hot-band kernel family.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: CTA grouping and orchestration overhead around the accepted PTX hot-band grouped-row mapping, not the hot-band inner K-stage itself.
candidates.risk: Medium. This is a narrow and already measured control on the restored surface, but it still changes launch-order locality and can regress if the current hybrid-prefetch base interacts differently with the older 4-row window.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the 4-row PTX grouping fails to beat the restored 25.47660828 ms control or if it reopens the old barrier/DRAM loss pattern.

candidates.direction_name: Port Grouped-Row Traversal Into The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling
ranking_notes: Historical run 20260420_184822_bf16_gemm_v1_2ab9365 measured 24.449024 ms, 0.271360 ms off the current accepted base 24.177664 ms.
Rehydrated from round_history because this family is absent from the current live frontier after the policy update.
Original diagnosis diagnosis_20260420_184122 ranked this direction at #1.
candidates.hypothesis: The latest measured run 20260420_184055_bf16_gemm_v1_9144f92 rejects the reopened 256x128 auxiliary branch as the next default path: the hot-band kernel alone took 40.086 ms versus 32.802 ms on the accepted 2e4dd24 base, tensor active dropped from 48.16% to 39.55%, barrier stalls jumped from 5.61% to 21.84%, and correctness failed on all 3 cases. The best next move is to keep the accepted grouped-row locality idea but remove the wide-CTA branch that introduced the barrier wall. The existing non-PTX 128x128 sibling is the cleanest control-family pivot because it already has correct measured ancestry, and porting the grouped-row physical-to-logical traversal into it is the most credible way to preserve locality without the broken 256x128 schedule.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: If the grouped-row port is incomplete, the sibling kernel will likely give back L2 reuse and shift the bottleneck from barrier stalls to long-scoreboard or memory-latency stalls. The target is lower barrier share without collapsing cache behavior relative to the accepted grouped PTX base.
candidates.risk: Medium. Historical measured evidence for the plain two-stage sibling is only mid-25 to high-26 ms, so this is not a proven winner by itself, but it is the lowest-risk new family after the latest 256x128 correctness failure and avoids repeating the same PTX inner-loop experiments that already regressed in rounds 3 through 5.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness pass rate across all 3 cases
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the grouped-row sibling still trails the accepted 24.44441605 ms base by more than about 1 ms without materially reducing hot-band barrier stalls versus the 9144f92 regression, or if it introduces any correctness failure.
candidates.notes: Historical rehydration from round_history run 20260420_184822_bf16_gemm_v1_2ab9365 (24.449024 ms) into the round 5/20 live queue.

candidates.direction_name: Continue The Active PTX One-K 128x128 Control-Path Exploit
ranking_notes: Round 4 already falsified grouped-CTA traversal as the primary next family on top of the current PTX winner.
This is the most coherent path that keeps the best-performing branch alive while targeting the still register-limited hot kernel.
It remains ahead of the historical restores because it attacks the live branch directly instead of rolling back to an older near-best state.
candidates.hypothesis: The grouped-CTA traversal retune just lost 0.341504 ms versus the 24.177664 ms accepted PTX base, which is a strong signal that CTA ordering is not the next high-leverage knob on this branch. The active kernel is still the PTX 128x128 hot-band microkernel at 200 registers per thread with only 16.60% active warps and 48.36% tensor-pipe activity, while DRAM remains low at 9.73%. The next best move is therefore to stay on the winning PTX branch and retune the inner control/export surface that actually governs issue efficiency: copy/consume order, helper lifetime, and export sequencing inside the live microkernel, not another grid-traversal experiment.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual PTX hot-band control-path overhead and live-range pressure inside the current 128x128 microkernel, not global bandwidth or another CTA-ordering miss.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This stays on the best measured family and touches the actual hot path, but PTX control-surface tweaks can still collapse into noise or silently grow live ranges if the scope is not bounded.

candidates.direction_name: Flatten PTX Hot-Band Compute Helpers To Reduce Register Pressure
ranking_notes: The register-limited signature stayed intact through the traversal regression, so this family remains well-supported by the current profile.
It is ranked below dir_01 because the direct control-path exploit is on the exact best branch today.
The historical rehydration keeps this family live even though it was displaced by the recent policy change.
candidates.hypothesis: The live PTX branch is still pinned at 200 registers per thread and only 2 resident CTAs per SM, so occupancy and latency hiding remain constrained even when the traversal mapping changes. A historically promising family at 24.696832 ms was to flatten the PTX hot-band accumulate/helper surface so ptxas can shorten live ranges, remove unnecessary helper abstraction, and maybe recover a few warps of residency without touching macro geometry. That makes register-pressure reduction the strongest non-restore secondary family now that the traversal probe has failed.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and weak latency hiding driven by helper structure and live-range expansion in the PTX hot-band compute path.
candidates.risk: Medium. The change is still local to the PTX microkernel, but helper flattening can easily become a no-op if the generated code and live ranges do not actually change.
